summary: ALT (SGPT) is the most specific marker of hepatocellular injury because it is a cytoplasmic enzyme found almost exclusively in hepatocytes.
AST is more specific for liver injury than ALT.
ALP is the most specific marker of hepatocellular injury.
An AST:ALT ratio greater than 2:1 suggests alcoholic hepatitis, because alcohol depletes pyridoxal phosphate (vitamin B6) needed for ALT synthesis.
An AST:ALT ratio less than 1 suggests alcoholic hepatitis.
The AST:ALT ratio is not useful in distinguishing alcoholic from viral hepatitis.
In primary hypothyroidism, TSH is elevated and free T4 is decreased because the thyroid fails to produce adequate hormone, removing negative feedback on the pituitary.
In primary hypothyroidism, both TSH and T4 are decreased.
In primary hypothyroidism, TSH is decreased while T4 is elevated.
In secondary hypothyroidism, both TSH and T4 are low because the pituitary fails to produce adequate TSH to stimulate the thyroid.
In secondary hypothyroidism, TSH is high and T4 is low.
Secondary hypothyroidism is caused by iodine deficiency.
Serum creatinine is the most commonly used marker of GFR because it is produced at a relatively constant rate from muscle metabolism and is freely filtered by the glomerulus.
Serum albumin is the best marker of glomerular filtration rate.
Blood urea nitrogen is more reliable than creatinine for estimating GFR.
Serum albumin reflects hepatic synthetic function and has a half-life of approximately 21 days, making it a marker of chronic rather than acute liver disease.
Albumin has a half-life of 3 days, making it useful for detecting acute liver injury.
Albumin is synthesised in the kidneys, not the liver.
Elevated ALP and GGT together suggest cholestatic (obstructive) liver disease, while elevated ALP alone may indicate bone disease.
Elevated ALP always indicates liver disease regardless of GGT levels.
GGT is specific for bone disease.
